claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my three children. 

Alex is 5 years old, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! Let me ask some follow-up questions about each of your children.

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Now, let me ask about your living arrangements with your children:

**Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Blake is 17 and Charlie is 19, I need to ask about their current education or training status.

**Is Blake (age 17) currently in approved education or training?** 

(Approved education includes A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications. Approved training includes unpaid training courses that aren't part of a job contract.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Blake is 17 and not in approved education or training, I have a follow-up question:

**Has Blake registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education or training?**

(If yes, Child Benefit can potentially continue for 20 weeks after they leave education or training.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have one more question about Blake:

**Does Blake work 24 hours or more a week, or receive any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work that many hours and isn't receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake.

Now let me ask about Charlie, who is 19:

**Is Charlie currently in approved education or training?**

(Approved education includes A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications. Approved training includes unpaid training courses that aren't part of a job contract.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is still in education. They're doing their A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just one more question about Charlie:

**Does Charlie work 24 hours or more a week, or receive any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work that many hours and isn't receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### **Summary**

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 5 | ✅ Yes |
| Blake | 17 | ❌ No |
| Charlie | 19 | ✅ Yes |

---

### **Detailed Reasoning**

**Alex (age 5)** – ✅ **Eligible**
Alex is under 16 years old, you live in the UK, and Alex lives with you. You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Blake (age 17)** – ❌ **Not Eligible**
Since Blake is over 16, they would need to be in approved education or training for you to continue receiving Child Benefit. Blake is not currently in education or training, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Therefore, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

**Charlie (age 19)** – ✅ **Eligible**
Although Charlie is 19, they are under 20 and remain in approved education (A-levels). Charlie does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in their own right. Therefore, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

---

If Blake's circumstances change (for example, if they enrol in approved education or training, or register with a careers service), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them at that point. 

Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, which is under 16. You live in the UK and Alex lives with you, meaning you are responsible for the child. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 17 years old, which means they would need to be in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit. Blake is not currently in any education or training, has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, and therefore does not meet the criteria for continuing Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over. As a result, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old but is still in approved education (studying A-levels). Charlie does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in their own right. You live in the UK and Charlie lives with you. Since Charlie is under 20 and remains in approved education, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and all three children live with you. Alex (age 5) qualifies as they are under 16. Charlie (age 19) qualifies because they are under 20 and still in approved education (A-levels). However, Blake (age 17) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and have not registered with a careers service or the armed services.

Blake is 17 years old, which means they would need to be in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit. Blake is not currently in any education or training, has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, and therefore does not meet the criteria for continuing Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over. As a result, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old but is still in approved education (studying A-levels). Charlie does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in their own right. You live in the UK and Charlie lives with you. Since Charlie is under 20 and remains in approved education, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

Charlie (age 19) qualifies because they are under 20 and still in approved education (A-levels). However, Blake (age 17) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and have not registered with a careers service or the armed services.
